name: Tanzania
tagline: From the footprints of Laetoli and the gold of Kilwa to Nyerere's union and a modern East African republic.
summary: Tanzania holds some of the oldest evidence of humankind on earth: at Laetoli, near Olduvai Gorge in the north, lie 3.6-million-year-old hominin footprints, while the gorge itself yielded the fossils and stone tools that made the Leakey family famous and the region a cradle of humanity. Over later millennia Bantu-speaking farmers settled the interior, and along the Indian Ocean shore a literate, Muslim Swahili civilisation arose, its greatest city — Kilwa Kisiwani — growing rich on the medieval gold trade. The Portuguese disrupted the coast after Vasco da Gama's voyage of 1498, and from the 1830s the Omani Arab sultanate of Zanzibar dominated it, building a clove-plantation economy on a brutal slave and ivory trade. Germany seized the mainland as German East Africa from the 1880s, crushing the Maji Maji rebellion of 1905–07 with a scorched-earth campaign that brought catastrophic famine; after the First World War Britain held mainland Tanganyika as a League of Nations mandate. Tanganyika won independence under Julius Nyerere and TANU on 9 December 1961, and after the Zanzibar Revolution of January 1964 the two states united on 26 April 1964 to form Tanzania, which Nyerere then steered toward Ujamaa socialism through the 1967 Arusha Declaration.

founding.label: Independence of Tanganyika from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: Mainland Tanganyika became an independent state on 9 December 1961, ending British rule that had begun as a League of Nations mandate after the First World War. Independence was won not by armed struggle but through constitutional negotiation led by Julius Nyerere and the Tanganyika African National Union (TANU), which had swept the territory's elections; Nyerere became prime minister, and on 9 December 1962 the country became a republic with him as its first president. The modern state of Tanzania was formed afterwards: following the Zanzibar Revolution of January 1964, Tanganyika and the islands of Zanzibar united on 26 April 1964 to create the United Republic of Tanzania, with Nyerere as president and Zanzibar's Abeid Karume as vice-president.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Tanganyika 9 December 1961 from the United Kingdom; republic 9 December 1962

quickFacts.label: Union
quickFacts.value: United Republic of Tanzania formed 26 April 1964 by the union of Tanganyika and Zanzibar

quickFacts.label: First president
quickFacts.value: Julius Nyerere (Mwalimu), leader of TANU, president 1962–1985

quickFacts.label: Human origins
quickFacts.value: Laetoli footprints, c. 3.6 million years old; Olduvai Gorge fossils and Oldowan stone tools

quickFacts.label: Swahili coast
quickFacts.value: Kilwa Kisiwani flourished on the gold trade in the 13th–15th centuries (UNESCO 1981)

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Dodoma (official); Dar es Salaam remains the largest city and commercial centre

eras.title: Cradle of Humankind: Olduvai and Laetoli
eras.period: c. 3.7 million years ago – c. 1000 BCE
eras.standfirst: The gorges and plains of northern Tanzania preserve some of the oldest traces of our ancestors anywhere on earth.
body: Few landscapes have shaped our understanding of human origins as profoundly as the northern Tanzanian plains around Olduvai Gorge, often called a cradle of humankind. From the 1930s the Kenyan-British palaeoanthropologists Louis and Mary Leakey searched the gorge's exposed beds, recovering stone tools and animal bones. In July 1959 Mary Leakey uncovered the robust hominin skull known as Zinjanthropus, later classified as Paranthropus boisei (specimen OH 5), a find that drew the world's attention to East Africa. In 1960 the Leakeys' team recovered fossils that became the type specimen of Homo habilis, the early toolmaker associated with the Oldowan pebble tools found in the gorge, the oldest of which date to roughly 1.8 million years ago.

Some 45 kilometres south of the gorge, at Laetoli, Mary Leakey's team made an even more startling discovery in 1976–78: a trail of fossilised footprints preserved in hardened volcanic ash and dated to about 3.6 million years ago. Attributed to Australopithecus afarensis — the species of the famous 'Lucy' skeleton — the prints show that these early hominins already walked upright on two legs, providing some of the clearest physical evidence for the antiquity of bipedalism. The footprints and the surrounding fossil sites lie within the Ngorongoro Conservation Area.

For hundreds of thousands of years after these distant ancestors, the lands that became Tanzania were home to hunter-gatherer communities. The earliest inhabitants of the interior are thought to have spoken Khoisan-related and later Cushitic languages, herding and foraging across the savanna long before the great migrations and trading civilisations that would transform the region in the last few thousand years.

events.title: Hominins leave footprints at Laetoli
events.summary: Early hominins, attributed to Australopithecus afarensis, cross a layer of fresh volcanic ash near Laetoli; their footprints harden and survive, giving direct evidence of upright walking.

events.title: Homo habilis and Oldowan tools at Olduvai
events.summary: Early members of the genus Homo at Olduvai Gorge make simple flaked-pebble tools of the Oldowan industry, among the earliest manufactured implements known.

events.title: Mary Leakey finds the Zinjanthropus skull
events.summary: At Olduvai Gorge, Mary Leakey uncovers the robust hominin skull OH 5 (Zinjanthropus / Paranthropus boisei), a landmark in the study of human evolution.

events.title: Discovery of the Laetoli footprints
events.summary: Mary Leakey's team excavates the trail of 3.6-million-year-old hominin footprints at Laetoli, confirming early bipedalism and cementing Tanzania's place in human prehistory.

figures.name: Mary Leakey
figures.blurb: British archaeologist whose discoveries at Olduvai Gorge — including the Zinjanthropus skull in 1959 — and her excavation of the Laetoli footprints in the 1970s transformed the study of human origins.

pullquote.text: The Laetoli footprints, frozen in volcanic ash for some 3.6 million years, are among the oldest direct traces of upright walking on earth.

eras.title: Bantu Settlers and the Swahili Coast
eras.standfirst: Iron-using Bantu farmers settled the interior while a wealthy, Muslim Swahili civilisation rose along the Indian Ocean shore.
body: From around the first millennium BCE, the great Bantu expansion brought iron-working, farming communities into what is now Tanzania, reaching the coast between the Rufiji delta and the Ruvu river by the close of that millennium. These migrations, layered with later movements of Nilotic and Cushitic peoples, produced the deep ethnic and linguistic diversity of the modern country. Bantu speakers carried with them ironworking traditions linked to the Urewe culture of the Great Lakes, one of Africa's oldest centres of iron smelting.

Along the Indian Ocean coast a distinctive civilisation took shape. From roughly the eighth century onward, coastal Bantu communities trading with merchants from Arabia, Persia and India gave rise to the Swahili: a literate, urban, Muslim culture whose language, Kiswahili, is a Bantu tongue enriched with Arabic vocabulary. Towns built in coral stone grew into largely independent city-states, and according to tradition a Persian, Shirazi, dynasty — said to descend from Ali ibn al-Hasan of Shiraz — founded the ruling house of Kilwa. Islam spread steadily among the coastal elites.

The greatest of these cities was Kilwa Kisiwani, on an island off the southern Tanzanian coast. Between the thirteenth and fifteenth centuries Kilwa flourished as it seized control of the gold trade flowing from the interior through its southern outpost of Sofala, minting its own coins and raising the Great Mosque, with its celebrated coral-stone domes — then among the largest mosques south of the Sahara. The Moroccan traveller Ibn Battuta, who visited in 1331, praised Kilwa as one of the most beautiful cities he had seen. The ruins of Kilwa and neighbouring Songo Mnara are today a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: Bantu farmers reach the coast
events.summary: Iron-using Bantu-speaking farmers settle the Tanzanian coast and interior, part of the wider Bantu expansion that reshaped eastern and southern Africa.

events.title: Rise of the Swahili coast
events.summary: Coastal communities trading across the Indian Ocean develop the Swahili language and an Afro-Islamic urban culture, founding settlements that grow into trading city-states.

events.title: Shirazi dynasty founds Kilwa
events.summary: According to the Kilwa Chronicle, the Shirazi line founds the ruling house of Kilwa Kisiwani; the city begins its rise as a Swahili trading power.

events.title: Kilwa dominates the gold trade
events.summary: Controlling the gold exported through Sofala, Kilwa reaches its peak of wealth, minting coins and building the Great Mosque, the largest in the region.

events.title: Ibn Battuta visits Kilwa
events.summary: The traveller Ibn Battuta describes Kilwa as a prosperous, devout and beautifully built Muslim city, attesting to the wealth of the medieval Swahili coast.

pullquote.text: Kilwa is one of the most beautiful and well-constructed towns in the world.

eras.title: The Portuguese and Omani Zanzibar
eras.standfirst: European intrusion and then Omani Arab dominance reshaped the coast, with Zanzibar becoming the hub of a clove economy built on the slave and ivory trade.
body: In 1498 the Portuguese navigator Vasco da Gama, seeking a sea route to India, reached the East African coast. The Portuguese soon used superior firepower to disrupt the rivalrous Swahili city-states, exacting tribute from Kilwa and others and establishing footholds at Mombasa, Kilwa and beyond. Their dominance hastened the decline of Kilwa and the older coastal order, though their hold on the coast was always contested and never deeply rooted.

From the late seventeenth century Omani Arabs displaced the Portuguese north of Mozambique, and over the following century the coast and its trade fell increasingly under Omani influence. In 1840 the Omani ruler Said bin Sultan moved his capital from Muscat to Zanzibar, recognising the islands' commercial potential. He promoted clove plantations worked by enslaved labour until Zanzibar and Pemba became the world's leading clove producers, and made the sultanate the dominant commercial power of the western Indian Ocean. When Said died in 1856 his domains were divided, and Zanzibar became a separate sultanate.

Zanzibar's wealth rested heavily on a brutal trade in enslaved Africans and ivory. Caravans reached deep into the interior — as far as Lake Tanganyika — and tens of thousands of captives were marched each year to coastal entrepôts such as Bagamoyo and shipped to Zanzibar's notorious slave market. This trade caused immense suffering and depopulation across the region. Under sustained British pressure, Sultan Barghash signed a treaty in 1873 prohibiting the seaborne slave trade and closing the Stone Town market, though slavery within the islands persisted for decades more.

events.title: Vasco da Gama reaches the coast
events.summary: The Portuguese navigator Vasco da Gama arrives on the East African coast en route to India, opening an era of Portuguese intrusion that disrupted the Swahili city-states.

events.title: Said bin Sultan moves his capital to Zanzibar
events.summary: The Omani ruler Said bin Sultan transfers his capital from Muscat to Zanzibar, making the islands the commercial heart of the western Indian Ocean.

events.title: Clove plantations and the caravan trade
events.summary: Zanzibar and Pemba become the world's leading clove producers, while caravans reaching to Lake Tanganyika feed a vast and brutal trade in enslaved people and ivory.

events.title: Zanzibar becomes a separate sultanate
events.summary: On Said's death his domains are divided; his son Majid becomes the first sultan of Zanzibar, separating the islands from Oman.

events.title: Zanzibar bans the seaborne slave trade
events.summary: Under British pressure Sultan Barghash signs a treaty prohibiting the maritime slave trade and closing the Stone Town slave market, though domestic slavery endures for decades.

figures.name: Said bin Sultan
figures.blurb: Omani ruler who moved his capital to Zanzibar in 1840, built its clove-plantation economy and made it the dominant commercial power of the western Indian Ocean, while presiding over its slave and ivory trade.

pullquote.text: By the 1840s Zanzibar had become the principal power in eastern Africa and the commercial capital of the western Indian Ocean.
pullquote.attribution: Summary of Said bin Sultan's sultanate

eras.title: German East Africa and the British Mandate
eras.standfirst: Germany seized the mainland and crushed the Maji Maji rebellion; after the First World War Britain ruled Tanganyika under a League of Nations mandate.
body: From the mid-1880s Germany claimed the mainland. The adventurer Carl Peters signed treaties with local rulers, and in 1885 the German government granted his company an imperial charter, the basis of the colony of German East Africa, which covered present-day Tanzania (excluding Zanzibar) together with Rwanda and Burundi. German rule was harsh, imposing taxes, forced labour and compulsory cotton cultivation on African communities.

These exactions provoked the Maji Maji rebellion of 1905–07, one of the largest anti-colonial uprisings in eastern Africa. Inspired by the prophet Kinjikitile Ngwale, whose followers believed a sacred water (maji) would turn German bullets to water, the revolt spread across the southeast. The Germans suppressed it with a scorched-earth campaign that destroyed crops and villages; the resulting famine, remembered as the Great Hunger, killed an estimated 75,000 to 300,000 people, overwhelmingly through starvation. Scholars have described the policy as genocidal.

During the First World War British and allied forces occupied German East Africa after a long campaign. By the Treaty of Versailles (1919) Germany lost the colony; Britain took the greater part as the Tanganyika Territory, administered from 1922 as a League of Nations mandate and later a United Nations trust territory. British rule was less developmental than extractive, but it left the mainland on a path toward the constitutional politics that would carry it to independence.

events.title: Germany establishes German East Africa
events.summary: Following Carl Peters's treaties, the German government charters his company, founding the colony of German East Africa over the Tanzanian mainland and the Great Lakes territories.

events.title: Maji Maji rebellion
events.summary: Inspired by the prophet Kinjikitile Ngwale, southern peoples rise against German rule, forced labour and cotton-growing; the revolt spreads widely before being crushed.

events.title: Scorched earth and the Great Hunger
events.summary: German forces suppress the rebellion by destroying crops and villages; the ensuing famine kills tens to hundreds of thousands of people, a catastrophe scholars term genocidal.

events.title: Tanganyika becomes a British mandate
events.summary: After the First World War the Treaty of Versailles strips Germany of the colony; Britain administers the mainland as the Tanganyika Territory under a League of Nations mandate from 1922.

figures.name: Kinjikitile Ngwale
figures.role: Spirit medium and leader of the Maji Maji rebellion
figures.blurb: Spirit medium whose teaching of a protective sacred water (maji) galvanised the 1905 uprising against German rule; he was captured and executed early in the revolt, which continued in his name.

pullquote.text: The war was followed by the Great Hunger, a famine that killed far more people than the fighting itself.
pullquote.attribution: On the suppression of the Maji Maji rebellion

eras.title: Independence, Revolution and the Union
eras.standfirst: Nyerere and TANU led Tanganyika to independence; the Zanzibar Revolution and a union created Tanzania, which then embraced Ujamaa socialism.
body: In 1954 the teacher Julius Nyerere — known to Tanzanians as Mwalimu, 'the teacher' — transformed an existing association into the Tanganyika African National Union (TANU), which campaigned for independence through peaceful, constitutional means. TANU swept the territory's elections, and on 9 December 1961 Tanganyika became independent, with Nyerere as prime minister; a year later, on 9 December 1962, it became a republic with him as its first president. Independence had been achieved without the armed struggle seen elsewhere in Africa.

The islands followed a more violent path. Zanzibar gained independence from Britain in December 1963 under its Arab-led sultanate, but on 12 January 1964 the African majority rose in the Zanzibar Revolution, overthrowing the sultan and the Arab-dominated government and establishing a republic led by Abeid Karume. The upheaval was bloody. Within months Nyerere and Karume agreed to merge their states: on 26 April 1964 Tanganyika and Zanzibar united to form the United Republic of Tanzania, with Nyerere as president and Karume as vice-president, while Zanzibar retained substantial autonomy and its own government.

Nyerere set out to build a distinctive African socialism. In the Arusha Declaration of 5 February 1967 he proclaimed Ujamaa — 'familyhood' — committing Tanzania to socialism, self-reliance and equality, nationalising key sectors and launching a programme of collective villages (ujamaa vijijini). The vision was idealistic and won Tanzania international respect, but in practice the villagisation drive, increasingly coercive, disrupted farming and failed to deliver the prosperity it promised.

events.title: Founding of TANU
events.summary: Julius Nyerere builds the Tanganyika African National Union, which campaigns for independence by constitutional means and comes to dominate the territory's politics.

events.title: Tanganyika gains independence
events.summary: Mainland Tanganyika becomes independent from Britain under Nyerere and TANU; it becomes a republic with Nyerere as president on 9 December 1962.

events.title: Zanzibar Revolution
events.summary: The African majority overthrows the Arab-led sultanate of Zanzibar in a bloody uprising, establishing a republic under Abeid Karume.

events.title: Union of Tanganyika and Zanzibar
events.summary: Tanganyika and Zanzibar unite to form the United Republic of Tanzania, with Nyerere as president and Karume as vice-president; Zanzibar keeps its own government.

events.title: The Arusha Declaration
events.summary: Nyerere proclaims Ujamaa, committing Tanzania to socialism and self-reliance, nationalising key sectors and launching the policy of collective villages.

figures.name: Julius Nyerere
figures.role: First president of Tanzania; founder of TANU
figures.blurb: Known as Mwalimu ('the teacher'), he led Tanganyika to independence in 1961, forged the union with Zanzibar in 1964, and shaped the nation through his philosophy of Ujamaa socialism set out in the 1967 Arusha Declaration.

figures.name: Abeid Amani Karume
figures.role: Revolutionary leader; first vice-president of Tanzania
figures.blurb: Led the Zanzibar Revolution of January 1964, became president of Zanzibar, and joined Nyerere in the union of April 1964, serving as the first vice-president of Tanzania.

pullquote.text: Socialism and self-reliance: the Arusha Declaration set out Nyerere's vision of Ujamaa, an African socialism rooted in the family and the village.

eras.title: Modern Tanzania
eras.standfirst: After the Ujamaa years Tanzania moved peacefully to multiparty democracy and steady growth, becoming one of East Africa's most stable nations.
body: By the early 1980s the Ujamaa model had run aground: collectivisation had ended around 1977–78, agricultural output had stagnated and the economy was in crisis. In 1985 Nyerere voluntarily stepped down — a rare peaceful handover in the Africa of his day — and was succeeded by Ali Hassan Mwinyi, who began to liberalise the economy. Tanzania abandoned its one-party socialist path, reintroducing multiparty politics in 1992 and holding its first multiparty elections in 1995, won by Benjamin Mkapa.

Tanzania has since remained among the more stable countries in eastern Africa, avoiding the civil wars that struck several neighbours, though the union with Zanzibar — which retains its own president and assembly — has at times been a source of political tension. The official capital was designated as Dodoma, in the centre of the country, while Dar es Salaam has remained the largest city and main commercial hub. The economy has grown steadily, driven by agriculture, mining, tourism centred on the country's celebrated wildlife parks and Mount Kilimanjaro, and the port trade of the coast.

In the twenty-first century power has continued to pass through regular elections, dominated by the ruling Chama Cha Mapinduzi (the party descended from TANU). President John Magufuli, elected in 2015, pursued an assertive anti-corruption and infrastructure agenda before his death in March 2021, when Vice-President Samia Suluhu Hassan succeeded him to become Tanzania's first female head of state. Tanzania today honours Nyerere's legacy of national unity and a single Swahili national language, holding together a country of great ethnic, religious and geographic diversity.

events.title: Nyerere steps down
events.summary: Julius Nyerere voluntarily retires from the presidency, handing power to Ali Hassan Mwinyi in a peaceful transition and opening the way to economic liberalisation.

events.title: Return to multiparty politics
events.summary: Tanzania amends its constitution to permit multiple parties, ending decades of one-party rule; the first multiparty elections follow in 1995.

events.title: Dodoma confirmed as capital
events.summary: Tanzania advances the long-planned move of its capital to the central city of Dodoma, while Dar es Salaam remains the largest city and commercial centre.

events.title: Samia Suluhu Hassan becomes president
events.summary: On the death of President John Magufuli, Vice-President Samia Suluhu Hassan is sworn in as the first female president of the United Republic of Tanzania.

pullquote.text: Nyerere's choice to step down in 1985 set a precedent of peaceful transfers of power that has helped keep Tanzania stable.

figures.name: Julius Nyerere
figures.blurb: Mwalimu ('the teacher') led Tanganyika to independence in 1961, united it with Zanzibar to form Tanzania in 1964, and pursued Ujamaa socialism set out in the 1967 Arusha Declaration before stepping down peacefully in 1985.

figures.name: Mary Leakey
figures.blurb: Her discoveries at Olduvai Gorge and her excavation of the 3.6-million-year-old Laetoli footprints made northern Tanzania central to the study of human origins.

figures.name: Said bin Sultan
figures.blurb: Moved his capital to Zanzibar in 1840 and built its clove economy and Indian Ocean commercial dominance, sustained by a brutal slave and ivory trade.
